turns.content: yá'át'ééh. i need some help thinking through a gift idea. my husband's got a big anniversary coming up with his work and i want to do something meaningful
Yá'át'ééh! I'd love to help with that. What kind of work does he do, and what's the anniversary?

raymond is a silversmith. he's been doing it thirty years now. thirty years of sitting in that workshop with his torch and his files making beautiful things out of silver and turquoise. his squash blossom necklaces, you know, those sell really well at the gallup ceremonial. people line up for his work

Thirty years of silversmithing is a remarkable milestone, and having a reputation at Gallup Ceremonial says a lot about the quality of his work. That's a real legacy. What kind of thing were you thinking — something related to his craft, something personal, or something completely different?

that's where i'm stuck. the man makes jewelry for a living so i can't exactly buy him a bracelet. he'd just look at it and tell me the bezel setting is off or something. you know how it is with craftsmen. they can't turn it off

Ha, that's the challenge of being married to someone who's mastered their craft — anything in their medium is going to get the professional eye. A few directions to think about:

1. **Something for the workshop** — a high-quality tool he wouldn't buy himself, or an upgrade to his setup. After 30 years he knows exactly what he needs but might not splurge on it.

2. **Documentation of his work** — a professional photo book of his best pieces over the years. If he doesn't already have one, that's both personal and useful for shows.

3. **Experience, not object** — a trip somewhere meaningful, maybe to see other artists' work or to source stones.

What resonates? You know him better than anyone.

the photo book idea is really good actually. he's never done anything like that and i know some of his early pieces are long gone, sold at markets before he had a name. but i have photos of a lot of them. i used to take pictures of everything before it went out the door. he'd roll his eyes but i did it anyway

That's perfect — you've been building the archive without even knowing it. A book showing the evolution of his work over thirty years would be incredibly special. He can see how his style developed, and pieces he probably forgot about would be right there.

For putting it together, you could use a service like Artifact Uprising or Blurb that lets you upload photos and arrange them in a hardcover book. You could organize it by era or by type — necklaces, bracelets, rings — and add short notes if you want. Even just dates and where the piece ended up.

How many photos do you think you have?

oh hundreds probably. some on my phone, some printed out in a box somewhere. this is going to be a project. but he's worth it. thirty years in that workshop, you know, his hands are like a map of every piece he's ever made. burns and calluses and silver dust in every crease. i tell him to wear gloves and he just looks at me

That image says everything about the man and his dedication. And the book doesn't have to include every photo — pick the strongest ones from each period, maybe 50-80 images, and let them tell the story. Quality over quantity.

Start by gathering everything into one place — pull the box, save the phone photos to a folder. Once you can see what you have, the organization will come naturally. You've got a couple weeks if the anniversary is coming up, or more time if you need it?

Either way, he's going to love it. A man who rolls his eyes at you photographing his work is going to pretend he's not emotional when he sees thirty years of it bound in a book.

ha. he'll say something like 'why'd you waste money on this' and then i'll catch him looking through it at the kitchen table every night for a week. i know my husband. alright i'm going to start digging through those photos tonight. ahéhee'

That sounds exactly right. Enjoy the dig through the photos — it'll probably be a trip down memory lane for you too. Ahéhee' for sharing this, and happy thirty years to Raymond. The man clearly chose the right partner.
